figures.caption: Fig. 2: Overview of the BeyondDrive framework. In Stage 1, classifier-free guidance and noise standard deviation scaling are employed to generate diverse trajectory candidates near expert demonstrations, followed by safety-aware and distance-aware filtering to construct hard negative samples that are unsafe yet expert-proximate. In Stage 2, the imitation learning loss and repulsive distance loss jointly optimize the policy to learn expert strategies while avoiding unsafe behaviors.
Table 3: Zero-shot Performance on the HUGSIM Benchmark.
Table 2: Performence on the NAVSIMv2 navtest Benchmark.
Fig. 7: Case studies on the HuGSIM benchmark. The ego vehicle avoids a truck, overtakes a bus, and merges into the main road in a closed-loop environment.

Fig. 8: Case studies on the HUGSIM benchmark. The ego vehicle passes a pedestrian crossing, maneuvers around a stationary car on the roadside, and returns to its current lane in a closed-loop environment.
Table 7: Ablation studies on different loss weights.
Fig. 12: Comparison of planning performance in a right-turn scenario.
Fig. 11: Comparison of planning performance in a left-turn scenario.
Table 9: Zero-shot Performance on the HUGSIM Benchmark.
Table 1: Performance on the NAVSIMv1 navtest Benchmark. "C" dontes Camera, and "L" denotes Lidar. * Indicates the optimized version.
Fig. 4: Impact of λrd/λimi on model performance.
Fig. 5: (a) Zero-value counts of hard negative samples. (b) Error distribution between hard negative samples and expert demonstrations. (c) Metric correlations of hard negative samples.
Fig. 10: Comparison of planning performance in a straight driving scenario.

Fig. 3: Case studies on the navtest benchmark. Left: front-view camera images. Middle: BEV scenes with expert demonstrations and LTF* planned trajectories (with scores). Right: expert demonstrations, LTFv7 planned trajectories and negative samples.

Table 4: Ablation studies on core components for both uni-modal and multi-modal methods. Hyperparameter Tuning includes replacing the constant scheduler with a cosine annealing scheduler with warmup, as well as adjusting the loss weights. Normalization is performed by using first-order differences of coordinates instead of raw coordinates.
Fig. 9: A failure case on the HUGSIM benchmark. In a meeting scenario, the oncoming vehicle swerves into the ego lane, and the ego vehicle fails to avoid the collision.

Table 6: Ablation studies on core components on the NAVSIMv2 navtest Benchmark. Ms1 denotes the fine-tuned hyperparameters of the uni-modal method LTF; Ms2 represents Ms1 with normalization; LTFv7 is derived by further incorporating BeyondDrive into Ms2. Mm1 denotes the application of BeyondDrive to the multimodal method DiffusionDrive, and Mm2 denotes the application of BeyondDrive to the multi-modal method MeanFuser. Mw1 denotes the application of BeyondDrive to the world model method WoTE.
